turns.speaker: model_a
turns.content: So what have you been up to lately?

turns.speaker: model_b
turns.content:
Honestly, a weird mix of classes, basketball, and trying to stay sane.

turns.content:

I've been listening to a ton of true crime podcasts lately instead of music - probably weird but I'm obsessed. Just finished this series about the Golden State Killer and it was insane.

Been working extra shifts at the gas station too since it's getting closer to the holidays. People are already buying way too much junk food and energy drinks. Yesterday this guy bought like 8 Red Bulls and I was like... you good bro?

turns.speaker: model_b

As for albums, my friends keep trying to die on the hill that *certain* Drake projects are untouchable, and I’m like… come on now, be serious. Also people overhype some stuff just because it’s nostalgic or easy to quote. I’m not saying they’re bad, just not the masterpiece people act like.

turns.content:

The Red Bull guy was definitely something else. I see all kinds of weird stuff working nights at the gas station. turns.speaker: model_b
turns.content:
Yeah, fair catch — I worded that sloppy. I’m at a four-year, and I’m in upper-level econ classes now, not literally doing gen eds as a senior. I meant the workload is still annoying in that “why am I doing problem sets at midnight” way. That’s on me.

And facts, *Views* is exactly the kind of album people pretend is deeper than it is. Drake has hits, obviously, but some of his stuff gets treated like scripture just because everybody had it on repeat in high school.

Criminal justice makes sense if you’re into true crime, but yeah, I’ve heard the same thing — lots of people get into it and then realize the job options are narrower than they thought. Still, if you actually like the subject, that’s worth something. Better than picking something random and hating your life for four years.

The gas station stories are hilarious though. Cat food and lottery tickets at 2 a.m. is such a specific kind of chaos. That’s the kind of thing that makes a shift feel like a fever dream.

And yeah, I’ve heard of *My Favorite Murder* — I’ve only listened to a few episodes, but Karen and Georgia are funny. I like that they don’t do the super creepy exploitative vibe some true crime stuff has. Do you have a favorite episode or case they covered?

turns.content:
Oh okay that makes way more sense! Yeah midnight problem sets are the worst - I feel like all my hardest assignments end up being due at weird times too. Community college is probably easier in some ways but the scheduling is still brutal when you're working.

I don't have like one favorite MFM episode but I really liked when they covered the Zodiac killer - they did a good job breaking down all the weird cipher stuff without getting too into conspiracy theory territory. And their episode on BTK was wild, like how did that guy just live a normal suburban life for so long? That's what gets me about true crime - it's always the people you'd never suspect.

Yeah the exploitative stuff really bugs me. Like some podcasts just seem to get off on the gore and don't care about the victims at all. MFM at least tries to be respectful and they're not afraid to call out bad police work or victim blaming.

Cat food lady came back again last night actually! Same routine - bunch of lottery tickets and Fancy Feast. I'm starting to think she's got like 15 cats or something. Either that or she's really committed to some weird ritual lol.

You working anywhere while you're in school or just focusing on classes? Four-year tuition is no joke, especially if you're not getting much financial aid.

turns.speaker: model_b
turns.content:
Yeah, I’m working part-time at a coffee shop near campus. Nothing glamorous, but it covers food and gas and keeps me from being completely broke. The schedule is annoying sometimes, but at least it’s flexible around classes and pickup.

And yeah, Zodiac and BTK are both crazy because they show how long somebody can hide in plain sight. That’s the part that always messes with me too — not the “movie villain” stuff, just how ordinary they can seem.

Also, the cat food lady is definitely either feeding a small army of cats or running some kind of low-stakes side quest every night. Fancy Feast plus lottery tickets is such a specific combo.

You seem pretty into the true crime side of it, though. Do you listen to any other podcasts besides MFM?

turns.speaker: model_a
turns.content:
Yeah I listen to a bunch! Serial was like the gateway drug for me - that first season about Adnan Syed was so good. I also really like Crime Junkie even though some people think Ashley's voice is annoying. And there's this one called Small Town Murder that's hilarious but also really well researched.

Oh and I've been binge-listening to this newer one called The Murder Squad - it's hosted by actual detectives so they break down cases from like a professional perspective. Really cool to hear how they think about evidence and stuff.

Coffee shop work sounds way better than gas station honestly. At least you're not dealing with people buying weird stuff at 3am or having to clean the bathrooms every few hours. Plus free coffee probably? That alone would be worth it for me, I'm like addicted to caffeine.

Do you ever get regulars who are super particular about their orders? I feel like coffee people are either super chill or absolutely insane about how they want their drink made. No in between.

The cat food lady mystery continues though - I'm genuinely curious now if she ever actually wins anything on those scratch-offs. She's gotta be down like hundreds of dollars at this point but she keeps coming back. Maybe the cats are good luck charms or something lol.

turns.speaker: model_b
turns.content:
Yeah, coffee regulars are absolutely a thing. You get the super chill people, and then you get the ones who want a whole custom ritual like they’re commissioning art. Same order every day, but if one tiny thing is off they act like the world ended.

And yeah, free coffee is basically the only reason I survive those shifts. That and the fact that most of the customers are just students or people heading to work, so it’s not as chaotic as a gas station at 3 a.m.

I haven’t listened to *The Murder Squad* much, but that sounds interesting. I like when a podcast actually knows what it’s talking about instead of just narrating Wikipedia with spooky music in the background.

Also, *Serial* season one was huge. I remember everybody talking about it like it was the Super Bowl for podcasts. Did you ever have a phase where you were convinced one theory was definitely right, or did you stay pretty neutral on it?

turns.speaker: model_a
turns.content:
Oh man, I was SO convinced Adnan was innocent for like the first half of the season! Like Jay's story kept changing and the cell tower evidence seemed sketchy. But then by the end I was like... I honestly have no idea. Sarah Koenig did such a good job showing how messy real cases are compared to like Law & Order where everything wraps up neat.

I think that's what got me hooked on true crime - realizing how much stuff we just don't know and probably never will. It's frustrating but also fascinating? Like with the Hae Min Lee case, there's still so much that doesn't add up even after all these years.

Yeah The Murder Squad is really good for that - they don't just read off Wikipedia. They actually explain why certain evidence matters or doesn't matter, and they call out bad investigation work when they see it. Makes you realize how much TV gets wrong about how this stuff actually works.

The coffee shop sounds way more civilized than my gas station chaos. Although I bet you still get some characters. Do you ever have people try to study there for like 6 hours on one cup of coffee? I feel like that's gotta be a thing at college coffee shops.

Also I'm dying to know if cat food lady ever hits it big on those scratch-offs. It's become like my own little mystery now lol.

turns.speaker: model_b
turns.content:
Oh 100%, the “I have no idea” feeling is basically the whole appeal. Real cases are messy in a way TV never is. Same with the Adnan case — the more you learn, the less clean the answer feels.

And yeah, people absolutely camp out at coffee shops. There’s always one person with a laptop, three chargers, and one sad iced coffee who’s been there so long I start wondering if they’re paying rent. I respect the hustle but also… bro, buy another drink.

Cat food lady is honestly becoming a legend at this point. If she ever wins big, she deserves a documentary.

Random question: are you more into podcasts because of the storytelling, or because you like the detective/analysis side of it?

turns.speaker: model_a
turns.content:
Honestly, it's probably both but I think the detective side hooks me more. Like I love a good story, but what really gets me is trying to piece things together - looking at evidence, thinking about timelines, figuring out what doesn't make sense. 

I'm always pausing podcasts to be like "wait, that doesn't add up" or "why didn't they check that?" It's probably annoying if anyone's listening with me lol. My roommate thinks I'm weird for taking notes sometimes but I can't help it.

The storytelling part is important too though - like if a podcast is boring or the hosts are annoying, I'm not gonna stick with it no matter how interesting the case is. But yeah, I think I'm more drawn to the puzzle aspect than just hearing a scary story, you know?

What about you? Are you more into it for the story or do you like trying to solve stuff too? I feel like some people just want to be entertained and others want to play detective from their couch.

Also speaking of mysteries - I should probably start a betting pool on when cat food lady finally hits the jackpot. I'm thinking she's due for at least a $20 winner soon, the odds have gotta catch up eventually right?

turns.speaker: model_b
turns.content:
Yeah, I’m way more into the puzzle part too. The story matters, but if the logic doesn’t hold up I stop caring fast. I’m the same way with movies and music too honestly — if something is all vibe and no substance, I’ll still enjoy it for a minute but I won’t defend it like it’s genius.

Taking notes while listening is not weird at all. That’s just being locked in. Your roommate probably just doesn’t get the process.

And nah, the odds don’t really “catch up” in the way people want them to. Scratch-offs are brutal. Someone can lose forever and still keep thinking the next one is the one. That’s probably how the cat food lady keeps the dream alive.

Also, I’m curious now — what’s one true crime case or podcast episode that actually changed how you thought about a case?

turns.speaker: model_a
turns.content:
Oh that's a good question! There was this Case File episode about the Lindy Chamberlain case - you know, the "dingo ate my baby" one? I always thought it was like this joke or urban legend, but when I actually heard the full story it was so messed up.

Like this woman's baby actually DID get taken by a dingo, but everyone just decided she was lying because she didn't act "sad enough" or whatever. The media and police just completely destroyed her life for years. It really made me think about how much we judge people based on how they react to trauma, when there's no "right" way to grieve.

That case totally changed how I listen to other podcasts now. Like when they're talking about how a victim's family "acted suspicious" or whatever, I'm way more skeptical. People process horrible stuff differently and it doesn't mean they're guilty of anything.

Plus it showed me how wrong the justice system can get things, even when they're super confident. Makes you wonder about other cases where maybe they got it wrong but nobody wants to admit it.

What about you? Any cases that like totally shifted your perspective on something?

And yeah you're probably right about the scratch-offs. Poor cat food lady is probably just funding the state lottery at this point lol.

turns.speaker: model_b
turns.content:
Yeah, that case is a perfect example of how people can get it dead wrong because they’re judging someone’s emotions instead of the facts. That stuff is infuriating.

For me, cases that shift my perspective are usually the ones where the “obvious” narrative falls apart once you look at the timeline. It makes me way more skeptical of first impressions in general, not just true crime. Like I try not to assume the loudest theory is the right one.

Also, random but I feel like you’d be good at this kind of stuff if you ever did criminal justice or even law. You seem really into evidence and reasoning.

Question for you: do you ever get into music podcasts or are you strictly true crime? Because I can go on forever about albums too if you want to open that door.

I feel like people my age either act like nothing good came out after 2010 or they think everything from before they were born is automatically trash.

I'm somewhere in the middle I guess. Like I can appreciate older stuff but I'm not gonna pretend I was born in the wrong generation or whatever. And some new music is genuinely great, people just don't want to admit it because it's popular.

What kind of albums do you get into arguments about? Is it mostly hip-hop or do you branch out? I feel like hip-hop arguments get the most heated for some reason.

turns.speaker: model_b

Yeah, hip-hop arguments are definitely the most heated, but I branch out. I’m one of those people who’ll argue about rap, R&B, and occasionally indie stuff if somebody says something ridiculous.

My take is older music gets a lot of automatic respect, but newer music gets dismissed way too fast. Like, classic doesn’t always mean better, and popular doesn’t always mean bad. A lot of people just confuse “I heard it everywhere” with “it’s overrated,” which isn’t always fair.

And yeah, 5:45am is criminal. Get some sleep for real — early shifts are rough enough without being half-dead.

Before you crash though, I gotta ask: what’s one album you think people sleep on that actually deserves way more credit?

turns.speaker: model_a
turns.content:
Oh man, good question! I think people really sleep on Ctrl by SZA. Like it got popular but I don't think people realize how well-crafted it actually is. Every song flows into the next one perfectly and her vocals are insane, but people just remember the hits and don't listen to the whole thing as an album, you know?

Also anything by Kali Uchis - people know her features but her solo stuff is so good. Sin Miedo is like perfect front to back but nobody talks about it.

What about you? What's an album you think deserves way more respect?

Alright I really gotta crash now though - 5:45am is gonna come way too fast and my manager gets pissy if we're even like 2 minutes late opening. Plus I still gotta set my coffee maker for tomorrow morning because there's no way I'm functioning without caffeine at that hour.

Thanks for the good convo though! Always nice to meet someone else who actually thinks about music instead of just putting on whatever's trending.

Night!
